docs.title: Longevity Research Library
docs.text:
This is a curated research interface focused on longevity enhancement for a 74-year-old male.

Not medical advice

This repository summarizes research for education and clinician discussion. It does not diagnose, prescribe, or replace medical care.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text:
The goal is to maintain a practical, evidence-focused view of:

  • Interventions that may improve healthspan, function, and resilience in older men
  • Biomarkers worth tracking with a clinician
  • Areas where claims are promising but not yet proven
  • Safety issues, contraindications, and medication interactions
  • New high-quality research as it emerges

docs.title: Default evidence hierarchy
docs.text:
1. Human randomized controlled trials and meta-analyses
  2. Large prospective cohort studies
  3. Mechanistic human studies
  4. Translational animal studies with clear human relevance
  5. Expert reviews from reputable clinicians/researchers
  6. Hypothesis-generating or speculative material, clearly labeled

docs.title: Starting focus areas
docs.text:
• Exercise: resistance, aerobic, VO₂ max, balance, fall prevention
  • Protein, nutrition quality, frailty prevention, body composition
  • Sleep, circadian health, cognition, mood, social connection
  • Cardiometabolic risk: blood pressure, ApoB/LDL-C, insulin resistance
  • Bone, muscle, mobility, sarcopenia
  • Vaccination, infection resilience, cancer screening
  • Geroprotective compounds: rapamycin, metformin, GLP-1s, SGLT2 inhibitors, NAD precursors, senolytics — tracked cautiously
  • Diagnostics: DEXA, labs, CGM where appropriate, cardiovascular risk imaging where clinically justified

docs.title: Paper Repositories
docs.text:
Curated collections of papers and source material gathered from videos, articles, and other evidence reviews.

  • Doctor Alex — “7 Lifestyle Traps I See in ER Every Shift”

docs.title: Doctor Alex — “7 Lifestyle Traps I See in ER Every Shift”
docs.text:
Video: YouTube Uploader: Doctor Alex Video date: 2026-06-02 Repository created: 2026-06-04

This repository captures the eight papers listed in the video description, with PubMed/DOI provenance, open PDFs where a downloadable full-text PDF could be verified, and concise findings summaries.

docs.title: Repository inventory
docs.text:
• metadata.json — PubMed-derived metadata, abstracts, IDs, access notes, and PDF source URLs.
  • references.bib — BibTeX-style citation file keyed by PMID.
  • pdfs/ — downloaded original PDFs where an open/full-text PDF was verified.
  • Not every paper has a redistributable open PDF. For those, the repository keeps the PubMed/DOI record and access note rather than scraping or redistributing paywalled publisher copies.

docs.title: 1. Mediterranean diet and cardiovascular prevention
docs.text:
Paper: Estruch et al., “Primary Prevention of Cardiovascular Disease with a Mediterranean Diet Supplemented with Extra-Virgin Olive Oil or Nuts.” New England Journal of Medicine (2018). Video theme: Treat food as medicine. PubMed: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/29897866/ DOI: https://doi.org/10.1056/NEJMoa1800389 Repository PDF: no open PDF verified.

Findings: In the revised PREDIMED randomized trial, 7,447 adults aged 55–80 at high cardiovascular risk but without baseline cardiovascular disease were assigned to a Mediterranean diet supplemented with extra-virgin olive oil, a Mediterranean diet supplemented with mixed nuts, or low-fat diet advice. Over a median 4.8 years, major cardiovascular events occurred in 3.8% of the olive-oil group, 3.4% of the nut group, and 4.4% of controls. Adjusted hazard ratios were about 0.69 for olive oil and 0.72 for nuts versus control, supporting fewer major cardiovascular events with Mediterranean dietary patterns. Important caveat: the original report was withdrawn and republished because of randomization/protocol deviations, so the revised analyses adjust for those issues rather than relying only on perfect randomization.

docs.title: 2. Ultra-processed foods and adverse health outcomes
docs.text:
Paper: Lane et al., “Ultra-processed food exposure and adverse health outcomes: umbrella review of epidemiological meta-analyses.” BMJ (2024). Video theme: Treat food as medicine / avoid industrialized diets. PubMed: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/38418082/ DOI: https://doi.org/10.1136/bmj-2023-077310 Repository PDF: PMID38418082 PDF

Findings: This umbrella review synthesized 45 pooled analyses, including 13 dose-response and 32 non-dose-response associations, covering nearly 9.9 million participants. Higher ultra-processed food exposure was directly associated with 32 of 45 health parameters across mortality, cancer, mental, respiratory, cardiovascular, gastrointestinal, and metabolic outcomes. The strongest credibility categories supported associations with cardiovascular disease mortality, type 2 diabetes, prevalent anxiety outcomes, and common mental disorder outcomes. The paper is broad and useful for risk mapping, but much of the evidence is observational and many GRADE ratings were low or very low, so it supports risk association more strongly than definitive causation.

docs.title: 3. Exercise as an anti-inflammatory intervention
docs.text:
Paper: Gleeson et al., “The anti-inflammatory effects of exercise: mechanisms and implications for the prevention and treatment of disease.” Nature Reviews Immunology (2011). Video theme: Do hard things / regular movement. PubMed: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/21818123/ DOI: https://doi.org/10.1038/nri3041 Repository PDF: no open PDF verified.

Findings: This review explains how regular exercise can lower chronic inflammatory burden, partly by reducing visceral fat and the inflammatory adipokines it releases, and partly through repeated acute exercise bouts that induce a more anti-inflammatory cytokine environment. The paper distinguishes beneficial regular activity from extreme training loads, noting that elite-level heavy exertion can suppress immunity and increase infection susceptibility. Its practical implication is that consistent, moderate-to-vigorous activity helps prevent and treat diseases with inflammatory components, including metabolic and cardiorespiratory disease.

docs.title: 4. Successful aging and controllable midlife factors
docs.text:
Paper: Vaillant & Mukamal, “Successful aging.” American Journal of Psychiatry (2001). Video theme: Lifestyle trajectory / long-term self-responsibility. PubMed: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/11384887/ DOI: https://doi.org/10.1176/appi.ajp.158.6.839 Repository PDF: no open PDF verified.

Findings: This long follow-up study examined two male cohorts followed from adolescence for roughly 60 years or until death. It compared less-controllable predictors, such as parental social class, family cohesion, childhood temperament, ancestral longevity, major depression, and health at 50, with more modifiable predictors, including alcohol abuse, smoking, marital stability, exercise, BMI, coping mechanisms, and education. Variables assessed before age 50 predicted physical health, disability/death before 80, social supports, mental health, instrumental activities of daily living, and life enjoyment at ages 70–80. After accounting for the more controllable variables, depression was the only less-controllable factor that still predicted aging outcomes, suggesting substantial room for lifestyle and psychosocial factors to influence late-life health.

docs.title: 5. Social relationships and mortality risk
docs.text:
Paper: Holt-Lunstad, Smith & Layton, “Social relationships and mortality risk: a meta-analytic review.” PLoS Medicine (2010). Video theme: Remove isolation. PubMed: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/20668659/ DOI: https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pmed.1000316 Repository PDF: PMID20668659 PDF

Findings: This meta-analysis pooled 148 studies with 308,849 participants. Stronger social relationships were associated with a 50% greater likelihood of survival, and the association was consistent across age, sex, initial health status, cause of death, and follow-up duration. More complex measures of social integration had the strongest association with survival, while simple “living alone” measures were weaker. The authors conclude that social relationships predict mortality at a magnitude comparable with established risk factors.

docs.title: 6. Loneliness, social isolation, living alone, and mortality
docs.text:
Paper: Holt-Lunstad et al., “Loneliness and social isolation as risk factors for mortality: a meta-analytic review.” Perspectives on Psychological Science (2015). Video theme: Remove isolation. PubMed: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/25910392/ DOI: https://doi.org/10.1177/1745691614568352 Repository PDF: no open PDF verified.

Findings: This meta-analysis examined mortality risk associated with objective social isolation, subjective loneliness, and living alone. After adjustment for possible confounders, social isolation was associated with 29% higher odds of mortality, loneliness with 26% higher odds, and living alone with 32% higher odds. The effects were broadly consistent by gender, follow-up length, and region, but initial health status and participant age moderated some results. The paper reinforces that both perceived and actual isolation are biologically meaningful risk factors, not just quality-of-life concerns.

docs.title: 7. Healthy lifestyle factors and life expectancy in the US
docs.text:
Paper: Li et al., “Impact of Healthy Lifestyle Factors on Life Expectancies in the US Population.” Circulation (2018). Video theme: Imagine doctors don’t exist / own the basics. PubMed: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/29712712/ DOI: https://doi.org/10.1161/CIRCULATIONAHA.117.032047 Repository PDF: no open PDF verified.

Findings: Using long-term data from the Nurses’ Health Study and Health Professionals Follow-up Study, the authors modeled five low-risk lifestyle factors: never smoking, BMI 18.5–24.9, at least 30 minutes/day of moderate-to-vigorous physical activity, moderate alcohol intake, and high diet quality. Across up to 34 years and 42,167 deaths, adults with all five low-risk factors had much lower mortality than those with none: hazard ratio 0.26 for all-cause mortality, 0.35 for cancer mortality, and 0.18 for cardiovascular mortality. Estimated life expectancy at age 50 was 14.0 years longer for women and 12.2 years longer for men with all five factors versus none. The result is powerful but observational/model-based, so it should be read as an estimate of lifestyle-associated survival advantage rather than proof from a randomized trial.

docs.title: 8. Aerobic exercise, hippocampal size, and memory
docs.text:
Paper: Erickson et al., “Exercise training increases size of hippocampus and improves memory.” PNAS (2011). Video theme: Work on mobility / movement and brain health. PubMed: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/21282661/ DOI: https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.1015950108 Repository PDF: PMID21282661 PDF

Findings: In a randomized controlled trial of 120 older adults, aerobic exercise training increased anterior hippocampal volume by about 2%, effectively reversing roughly one to two years of age-related hippocampal volume loss. The increase was associated with improved spatial memory and higher serum BDNF, a mediator linked to neurogenesis. The control group’s hippocampal volume declined, while higher baseline fitness partly protected against decline. The study gives concrete neurobiological evidence that aerobic training can affect brain structure and memory-relevant function in late adulthood.

docs.title: Cross-paper synthesis
docs.text: The cited papers converge on a simple preventive-health pattern: diet quality, regular exercise, social connection, and avoidance of major behavioral risks are repeatedly associated with lower chronic disease burden and longer survival. The strongest designs here include randomized trials for Mediterranean diet and aerobic exercise, while several lifestyle and social-connection papers are observational or meta-analytic and therefore more vulnerable to confounding. Even with that caveat, the direction is consistent: small daily behaviors compound over years, and social and metabolic health are not separate domains.

docs.title: Access notes
docs.text: Downloaded PDFs were limited to sources that returned a verifiable PDF from an open/full-text endpoint. Paywalled or non-verified PDFs were not scraped or redistributed. See metadata.json for the access status and attempted source URL for each paper.

docs.title: Medical Safety Policy
docs.text: This repository is educational and research-focused. It must not provide personal medical advice.

docs.title: Required safety language
docs.text:
For interventions involving medications, hormones, procedures, supplements, or major diet/exercise changes, Hermes must include:

  • Not medical advice
  • Discuss with a qualified clinician
  • Possible contraindications/interactions
  • Monitoring considerations
  • Uncertainty level

docs.title: Prohibited automated recommendations
docs.text:
Hermes should not directly recommend that the user:

  • Start, stop, or change prescription medication
  • Use off-label geroscience compounds without medical supervision
  • Ignore symptoms or delay medical care
  • Replace clinician-directed screening or treatment
  • Adopt extreme diets, fasting, or exercise programs without safety context

docs.title: Preferred framing
docs.text:
Use:

  • “Worth discussing with a clinician”
  • “Evidence is promising but not definitive”
  • “Potential risks include…”
  • “This changes the watchlist/evidence map by…”

Avoid:

  • “You should take…”
  • “This will extend lifespan…”
  • “Proven anti-aging treatment”

docs.title: Selection Policy
docs.text: Hermes should include only research that is plausibly useful for longevity enhancement and healthy aging in a 74-year-old male.

docs.title: Include preferentially
docs.text:
• Human RCTs, meta-analyses, systematic reviews
  • Large prospective cohort studies with clinically meaningful outcomes
  • Research in older adults, especially men over 65, when available
  • Studies measuring function, morbidity, mortality, hospitalization, cognition, cardiovascular events, frailty, or quality of life
  • Reputable sources: peer-reviewed journals, arXiv/medRxiv/bioRxiv with caution labels, NIH/CDC/WHO/NIA, major academic centers

docs.title: Deprioritize or exclude
docs.text:
• Marketing pages, supplement-company claims, and affiliate content
  • Animal-only findings unless highly relevant and clearly labeled
  • Small uncontrolled biohacker anecdotes
  • Papers focused only on surrogate markers without clinical context
  • Duplicate preprints or minor updates unless important
  • Research with obvious conflicts of interest and weak methodology

docs.title: Scoring rubric
docs.text:
Score each candidate from 0 to 10:

  • Relevance to 74-year-old male: 0–2
  • Evidence quality: 0–3
  • Clinical/practical significance: 0–2
  • Novelty or field-changing value: 0–1
  • Safety clarity and applicability: 0–2

Default inclusion threshold: 7.5/10.

docs.title: Required note fields
docs.text:
Every included item must include:

  • Citation and URL
  • Date found
  • Evidence type
  • Population studied
  • Summary
  • Main finding
  • Limitations
  • Safety considerations
  • Relevance to this repository
  • Whether clinician discussion is warranted

docs.title: Scope
docs.text:
This dossier tracks research relevant to extending healthspan, preserving function, reducing disease risk, and improving resilience for a 74-year-old male.

It emphasizes practical clinical relevance over hype.

docs.title: Core objectives
docs.text:
• Preserve muscle mass, strength, balance, and independence
  • Reduce cardiovascular, metabolic, cognitive, cancer, fracture, and infection risks
  • Improve sleep, energy, cognition, and quality of life
  • Identify interventions with favorable benefit/risk profiles
  • Track emerging geroscience cautiously and critically

docs.title: Highest-confidence domains
docs.text:
• Progressive resistance training
  • Aerobic conditioning and VO₂ max maintenance
  • Balance/fall-prevention training
  • Adequate protein and nutrition quality
  • Blood pressure and lipid risk reduction
  • Sleep quality and sleep apnea evaluation when indicated
  • Vaccinations and infection-risk reduction
  • Bone density and fracture risk management
  • Medication review and deprescribing where appropriate

docs.title: Watchlist domains
docs.text:
• Rapamycin/rapalogs
  • Metformin in non-diabetics
  • GLP-1 receptor agonists and muscle preservation in older adults
  • SGLT2 inhibitors for non-diabetic aging-related outcomes
  • NAD precursors
  • Senolytics
  • Plasma/exosome/stem-cell claims
  • Epigenetic clocks and biological age testing

docs.title: Review stance
docs.text:
For each intervention, ask:

  1. Is there meaningful human evidence in older adults?
  2. Does it improve hard outcomes, validated biomarkers, or functional capacity?
  3. What are the risks for a 74-year-old male?
  4. What interactions or contraindications matter?
  5. Is this ready for clinician discussion, or only worth monitoring?

docs.title: Biomarkers & Measurement
docs.text:
Warning

Biomarkers should be interpreted by qualified clinicians in clinical context.

docs.title: Cardiometabolic
docs.text:
• Blood pressure, including orthostatic symptoms if relevant
  • Lipid panel, preferably including ApoB and Lp(a) at least once
  • HbA1c, fasting glucose, fasting insulin where appropriate
  • Kidney and liver function
  • hs-CRP when clinically useful

docs.title: Function and body composition
docs.text:
• Grip strength
  • Gait speed
  • Chair-stand performance
  • Balance tests
  • DEXA for bone density, and body composition if useful
  • Waist circumference

docs.title: Nutrition and deficiency risk
docs.text:
• Vitamin B12, especially if using metformin or with neuropathy/cognitive symptoms
  • Vitamin D when clinically indicated
  • Iron studies if anemia/fatigue is present

docs.title: Cognitive/sleep
docs.text:
• Sleep apnea screening when indicated
  • Cognitive baseline if concerns arise

docs.title: Experimental / cautionary markers
docs.text:
• Epigenetic clocks
  • Biological age panels
  • Telomere length
  • Broad inflammatory cytokine panels
  • Consumer microbiome tests

These may be interesting but should not drive major interventions without stronger evidence.

docs.title: Questions for Clinicians
docs.text: Use this as a discussion checklist, not as self-treatment guidance.

docs.title: Primary care / geriatric medicine
docs.text:
• Are cardiovascular risk factors optimally managed for my age, goals, and side-effect tolerance?
  • Are my vaccinations current?
  • Are cancer screenings appropriate given my age, health status, and preferences?
  • Should I have a structured medication review for deprescribing opportunities?
  • Should we screen for sleep apnea, osteoporosis, frailty, fall risk, hearing, vision, or cognition?

docs.title: Cardiology / metabolic health
docs.text:
• Should ApoB, Lp(a), CAC scoring, or other risk stratification be considered?
  • Are blood pressure targets appropriate, avoiding dizziness/falls?
  • Are glucose and insulin-resistance markers concerning?

docs.title: Exercise / physical therapy
docs.text:
• What resistance and balance program is safe and progressive for me?
  • Are there gait, strength, or mobility limitations that need supervised PT?

docs.title: Geroscience / longevity clinic questions
docs.text:
• Which recommendations are supported by human outcome data?
  • What are the risks, monitoring plan, and stopping criteria?
  • Could this intervention interact with current medications or conditions?
  • Are there clinical trials that are more appropriate than off-label use?

docs.title: Legend
docs.text:
• Strong: multiple human RCTs/meta-analyses, clinically meaningful outcomes
  • Moderate: some human trial evidence or strong observational evidence
  • Early: plausible but limited human evidence
  • Speculative: mostly animal/mechanistic, commercial hype, or unclear endpoints

docs.title: Exercise and function
docs.text:
• Resistance training: Strong
  • Aerobic training / VO₂ max: Strong
  • Balance and fall prevention: Strong
  • Power training for older adults: Moderate
  • Multidomain frailty interventions combining home/community exercise with nutrition, environmental, and daily-function supports: Moderate

docs.title: Nutrition and body composition
docs.text:
• Adequate protein intake: Moderate to Strong
  • Mediterranean-style dietary pattern: Strong
  • Creatine for older adults: Moderate
  • Aggressive calorie restriction in older adults: Risk-dependent; caution

docs.title: Cardiometabolic risk
docs.text:
• Blood pressure control: Strong
  • ApoB/LDL-C lowering when indicated: Strong
  • Diabetes prevention/management: Strong; long-term DPP/DPPOS follow-up supports intensive lifestyle intervention for lower multimorbidity burden in adults with prediabetes, whereas metformin did not show a comparable multimorbidity association in that analysis
  • SGLT2 inhibitors: Strong for indicated type 2 diabetes/heart failure/chronic kidney disease contexts; older-adult randomized-subgroup meta-analysis in non-dialysis CKD supports renal and heart-failure benefit while reinforcing monitoring for genital infections and volume depletion/hypotension; emerging observational data suggest no excess geriatric-syndrome signal in older adults with type 2 diabetes, and newer absolute-benefit models may help clinician-led targeting for primary heart-failure prevention in type 2 diabetes
  • GLP-1 receptor agonists: Strong for indicated obesity/diabetes contexts, with stronger disease-specific cardiorenal evidence in CKD/type 2 diabetes/ASCVD-risk contexts; monitor muscle/frailty implications and evolving safety signals including rare ocular events and mixed musculoskeletal/fall-fracture data

docs.title: Geroscience compounds
docs.text:
• Rapamycin/rapalogs: Early for longevity; stronger in specific disease contexts
  • Metformin for longevity in non-diabetics: Early/uncertain
  • NAD precursors: Early; biomarker effects do not prove outcome benefit
  • Senolytics: Early/speculative outside trials

docs.title: Diagnostics and tracking
docs.text:
• Standard labs and risk-factor tracking: Strong
  • Handgrip strength and simple physical-performance measures as risk markers: Moderate to Strong for risk stratification; not treatment-directing by themselves
  • Intrinsic-capacity / multidomain geriatric assessment concepts: Moderate for risk stratification; implementation should be clinician-guided; newer Health and Retirement Study evidence suggests dynamic deterioration or recovery in intrinsic capacity tracks later cognitive decline and dementia risk
  • Continuous glucose monitoring (CGM): Strong for selected insulin-treated type 2 diabetes contexts; not a general longevity tracker
  • DEXA for bone/body composition: Moderate to Strong depending on indication
  • Epigenetic clocks: Early; not yet treatment-directing; recent cohort evidence suggests conventional risk factors can outperform epigenetic clocks for chronic-disease prediction, and muscle-aging associations remain modest/sex-specific
  • Consumer biological age panels: Use cautiously

docs.title: Infection resilience
docs.text:
• Age-appropriate vaccination (for example influenza, COVID-19, RSV, pneumococcal, shingles per current clinical guidance): Strong for preventing infection-related morbidity in indicated older adults; recent real-world evidence in adults ≥60 supports adjuvanted RSVPreF3 effectiveness against RSV-related hospitalization during the 2023–2024 season
  • Combined influenza/COVID-19 mRNA vaccines: Promising/investigational product-specific evidence for durable immunogenicity; require authorization and outcome/surveillance data before treatment-directing use
  • Post-COVID cardiovascular and long-COVID vigilance: Moderate observational evidence for an early post-infection cardiovascular risk window, especially in older adults; newer target-trial emulation evidence does not show a meaningful third-dose effect on long-COVID incidence, so booster decisions should remain anchored to current severe-disease prevention guidance

docs.title: Intervention Watchlist
docs.text: This page tracks interventions that are either high priority or commonly promoted in longevity circles.

docs.title: Progressive resistance training
docs.text:
• Primary aim: preserve muscle mass, strength, glucose disposal, mobility, and independence.
  • Watch for: orthopedic limitations, recovery, supervision needs.
  • Evidence status: strong for function and healthy aging.

docs.title: Aerobic conditioning
docs.text:
• Primary aim: cardiovascular resilience, VO₂ max, metabolic health, cognition.
  • Watch for: cardiac symptoms, overtraining, joint limitations.
  • Evidence status: strong.

docs.title: Blood pressure and lipid optimization
docs.text:
• Primary aim: reduce cardiovascular and cerebrovascular risk.
  • Watch for: overtreatment, orthostatic hypotension, polypharmacy.
  • Evidence status: strong.

docs.title: Frailty screening and multidomain support
docs.text:
• Primary aim: identify early physical, cognitive, social, and multidimensional frailty risks before they translate into falls, hospitalization, disability, or loss of independence.
  • Why tracked: a 2026 PromeTheus randomized trial found modest improvements in function, SPPB, and frailty status from a 12-month home-based multifactorial program in prefrail/frail adults ≥70 years, while a 2026 umbrella review linked frailty and prefrailty to mortality, hospitalization, falls/fractures, cognitive decline, and institutionalization.
  • Watch for: unsafe unsupervised exercise in people with cardiovascular symptoms, high fall risk, severe osteoporosis, neuropathy, dizziness, or orthopedic limitations; undernutrition during activity increases; and medications that worsen orthostasis or sedation.
  • Evidence status: moderate for multidomain intervention effects on function/frailty status; strong as a risk-stratification concept.

docs.title: SGLT2 inhibitors when clinically indicated
docs.text:
• Primary aim: cardiorenal and metabolic risk reduction in standard indications such as type 2 diabetes, heart failure, or chronic kidney disease.
  • Why tracked: recent older-adult observational data suggest no excess geriatric-syndrome signal and lower hospitalization/mortality compared with DPP-4 inhibitors in type 2 diabetes.
  • Watch for: volume depletion, genital/urinary infections, ketoacidosis risk in susceptible settings, renal-function thresholds, perioperative/sick-day medication management, and orthostatic symptoms.
  • Evidence status: strong for indicated disease contexts; not a general longevity drug for people without indications.

docs.title: GLP-1 receptor agonists when clinically indicated
docs.text:
• Primary aim: obesity/diabetes management and cardiometabolic risk reduction in appropriate patients.
  • Why tracked: FLOW subgroup analyses and a 2026 CKD-restricted RCT meta-analysis reinforce cardiorenal benefit for GLP-1 receptor agonists in indicated CKD/type 2 diabetes/ASCVD-risk contexts. A 2026 post hoc analysis of SURMOUNT/SUMMIT trials found broadly comparable tirzepatide efficacy and safety in adults ≥65 with obesity, a 2026 phase 3 retatrutide trial showed large HbA1c and weight reductions in younger adults with type 2 diabetes, and a 2026 TriNetX older-adult diabetes cohort found lower falls and femoral fractures with semaglutide/tirzepatide versus DPP-4 inhibitors. Together these make incretin agonists an important watchlist area rather than a general longevity recommendation.
  • Watch for: gastrointestinal effects, dehydration, gallbladder/pancreatitis concerns, excessive lean-mass loss during weight reduction, frailty/nutrition issues, kidney-function context, retinopathy monitoring when relevant, mixed bone density/fall/fracture evidence, and rare ocular safety signals such as possible semaglutide-associated NAION/ischemic optic neuropathy; sudden vision changes warrant urgent clinician/eye-specialist evaluation.
  • Evidence status: strong for indicated obesity/diabetes/cardiorenal contexts, including disease-specific evidence in CKD; safety monitoring remains important in older adults and this is not a general longevity-drug endorsement.

docs.title: Rapamycin / rapalogs
docs.text:
• Why tracked: strong animal data and mechanistic rationale.
  • Human longevity evidence: not yet definitive.
  • Safety issues: immune effects, lipids, glucose, mouth ulcers, wound healing, dosing uncertainty.
  • Repository stance: monitor trials and high-quality reviews; discuss only with an experienced physician.

docs.title: Metformin
docs.text:
• Why tracked: diabetes outcomes, possible aging-related associations.
  • Human longevity evidence in non-diabetics: uncertain; 2026 DPP/DPPOS follow-up found lower multimorbidity risk with intensive lifestyle intervention but not metformin versus placebo among adults originally enrolled with prediabetes.
  • Safety issues: B12 deficiency, GI effects, renal function considerations, possible exercise adaptation concerns.

docs.title: NAD precursors
docs.text:
• Why tracked: mechanistic interest and commercial popularity.
  • Human outcome evidence: limited.
  • Repository stance: require clinically meaningful outcomes, not just NAD-level changes.

docs.title: Senolytics
docs.text:
• Why tracked: promising preclinical rationale.
  • Human evidence: early and indication-specific.
  • Safety issues: varies by compound; avoid DIY protocols.

docs.title: Spermidine
docs.text:
• Why tracked: mechanistic interest in autophagy and immune aging, plus a small older-adult pilot RCT suggesting possible improvement in COVID-vaccine response biomarkers among vaccine non-responders.
  • Human longevity evidence: early; current evidence is based on surrogate immune outcomes rather than infections, hospitalization, function, or mortality.
  • Safety issues: supplement quality and dose consistency, comorbidity interactions, renal/hepatic disease considerations, cancer/immunology contexts, and peri-procedural uncertainty.
  • Repository stance: monitor larger randomized trials with clinical infection or resilience outcomes; discuss with a clinician before use, especially with polypharmacy or chronic disease.

docs.title: Open Questions
docs.text:
Questions this repository should keep revisiting as new evidence appears:

  • Which interventions improve function and resilience specifically in adults over 70?
  • How should older adults balance muscle preservation with weight loss or metabolic therapies?
  • Do rapalogs improve clinically meaningful aging outcomes in humans?
  • Are any senolytic strategies ready outside clinical trials?
  • Which biomarkers are actually actionable rather than merely interesting?
  • What screening intensity maximizes benefit and minimizes overdiagnosis in healthy older men?
  • Which combinations of exercise, nutrition, and medications produce additive benefit versus unnecessary burden?
  • Which combinations of resistance, aerobic, balance, power, nutrition, and environmental support produce the best functional outcomes in men in their 70s and 80s?
  • How can clinicians identify older adults most likely to gain cardiorenal benefit from GLP-1/SGLT2 therapies while minimizing dehydration, orthostasis, sarcopenia, fracture, ocular, and polypharmacy risks?
  • Can geroscience-adjacent supplements such as spermidine improve clinically meaningful immune resilience outcomes in older adults, or only short-term vaccine-response biomarkers?
  • How should clinicians stratify rare ocular safety signals such as possible semaglutide-associated NAION risk in older adults with diabetes, sleep apnea, vascular disease, or prior eye disease?

docs.title: Research Updates
docs.text: This section contains dated updates produced by Hermes.

  • 2026-06-21 — Sarcopenia exercise meta-analysis, DPP/DPPOS lifestyle-vs-metformin multimorbidity follow-up, AHA older-adult heart-failure statement, tirzepatide older-adult obesity subgroup, GLP-1 fragility-fracture safety signal, and tACS cognitive-aging watchlist item.
  • 2026-06-14 — Resistance-training programming meta-analysis, handgrip/cognitive-decline meta-analysis, intrinsic-capacity disability cohort, retatrutide phase 3 diabetes watchlist item, and investigational RSV/hMPV vaccine trial.
  • 2026-06-08 — Home-based multifactorial frailty RCT, frailty-outcomes umbrella review, semaglutide FLOW diabetes/CKD subgroup analysis, Danish cardiometabolic drug-safety cohort, and older-adult influenza vaccine effectiveness.
  • 2026-05 Monthly Synthesis — Monthly synthesis covering SGLT2 inhibitor older-adult evidence, semaglutide ocular safety signal, dietary-pattern reinforcement, exercise cognition uncertainty, and spermidine vaccine-response watchlist status.
  • 2026-06-01 — Combined influenza/COVID-19 vaccine durability trial, CGM randomized trial in type 2 diabetes, SGLT2 absolute-benefit model, and COVID-19 post-infection cardiovascular risk cohort.
  • 2026-05-25 — SGLT2 geriatric-syndrome cohort, semaglutide NAION safety meta-analysis, anti-inflammatory diet target-trial emulation, online exercise RCT, and spermidine vaccine-response pilot.

docs.title: Update format
docs.text:
Each update should include:

  • New high-quality papers or reviews
  • Why each item matters for a 74-year-old male
  • Strength of evidence
  • Practical implications
  • Limitations and safety notes
  • Whether the evidence changes the existing evidence map

No automated update should recommend starting, stopping, or changing treatment without clinician review.

docs.title: Research Update — 2026-05-25
docs.text: Educational research support only; not medical advice. Items were screened against the repository selection rubric with emphasis on human evidence, older-adult applicability, clinical relevance, and safety clarity. Search window: approximately 2026-05-11 through 2026-05-25.

docs.title: 1. SGLT2 inhibitors and geriatric syndromes in older adults with type 2 diabetes
docs.text:
• Citation / URL: Lee C-Y, Fang T-J, Jen C-H, Chuang Y-S, et al. “SGLT2 inhibitors and geriatric syndromes in older adults with type 2 diabetes: A real-world cohort study.” Journal of the Formosan Medical Association. Published 2026-05-22. PMID: 42173784. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/42173784/
  • Date found: 2026-05-25
  • Evidence type: Large propensity-score-matched real-world cohort study.
  • Population studied: Adults aged ≥65 with type 2 diabetes newly initiated on SGLT2 inhibitors or DPP-4 inhibitors; after matching, 42,440 people per group, mean age 71.4 years, 60% male.
  • Repository score: 8.5/10 — high relevance to older men with cardiometabolic disease, large human comparative dataset, clinically meaningful outcomes; downgraded because it is observational and indication-specific.
  • Summary: The study evaluated whether SGLT2 inhibitors were associated with geriatric syndromes, hospitalization, and mortality in older adults with type 2 diabetes.
  • Main finding: Compared with DPP-4 inhibitor initiation, SGLT2 inhibitor initiation was associated with lower risks of several geriatric outcomes, all-cause hospitalization at 5 years (HR 0.81, 95% CI 0.78–0.83), and all-cause mortality at 5 years (HR 0.65, 95% CI 0.62–0.69). Results were reported as consistent across sex, age, HbA1c, heart disease, and renal-function subgroups.
  • Relevance to a 74-year-old male: Highly relevant if type 2 diabetes, heart failure, chronic kidney disease, or other clinician-recognized indications are present. It is not evidence for SGLT2 inhibitors as a general longevity drug in people without standard indications.
  • Limitations: Observational design; residual confounding and prescribing-channel bias are possible. Outcomes were based on electronic health-record coding. The comparator was DPP-4 inhibitors, not placebo or lifestyle care.
  • Safety considerations: Not medical advice. SGLT2 inhibitors can be associated with genital/urinary infections, volume depletion, hypotension, ketoacidosis risk in susceptible settings, perioperative sick-day issues, and renal-function considerations. Clinician review and monitoring are necessary.
  • Clinician discussion warranted: Yes, if there is an existing cardiometabolic indication or medication-review question.
  • Evidence-map change: Yes. Added SGLT2 inhibitors under cardiometabolic risk as strong for indicated cardiorenal/metabolic disease with emerging reassuring geriatric-syndrome observational data.

docs.title: 2. Semaglutide and nonarteritic anterior ischemic optic neuropathy risk
docs.text:
• Citation / URL: Chrzanowski J, Walicka M, Burzyński J, Zaraś M, Michalak A, Fendler W, et al. “Semaglutide-associated risk of nonarteritic anterior ischemic optic neuropathy in patients with type 2 diabetes: A systematic review and meta-analysis of observational studies.” PLOS Medicine. Published 2026-05-21. PMID: 42166479. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/42166479/
  • Date found: 2026-05-25
  • Evidence type: Systematic review and meta-analysis of observational studies.
  • Population studied: Patients with type 2 diabetes treated with semaglutide compared with other glucose-lowering regimens; five eligible observational studies in the primary analysis, with seven additional studies in sensitivity analyses.
  • Repository score: 8.0/10 as a safety/watchlist item — clinically important safety signal for a widely used cardiometabolic intervention; downgraded for low certainty and observational evidence.
  • Summary: The review examined whether semaglutide is associated with nonarteritic anterior ischemic optic neuropathy (NAION), a rare but potentially vision-threatening optic neuropathy.
  • Main finding: Semaglutide use was associated with higher relative hazard of NAION versus non-semaglutide glucose-lowering regimens (HR 2.17, 95% CI 1.73–2.74). The estimated absolute risk remained low: 0.014% per year, roughly one additional NAION case per 7,000 semaglutide-treated patients annually. Certainty was graded as low.
  • Relevance to a 74-year-old male: Relevant because GLP-1 receptor agonists are common in older adults with diabetes, obesity, or cardiovascular-risk indications. Older age, diabetes, sleep apnea, vascular risk factors, and optic-nerve vulnerability may affect baseline risk.
  • Limitations: Registry-based observational evidence; possible outcome misclassification and confounding by indication/disease severity. The finding does not establish causality and does not quantify risk for all GLP-1 drugs or people without diabetes.
  • Safety considerations: Not medical advice. This is a clinician-discussion safety signal, especially for people with prior NAION, visual symptoms, optic-nerve risk factors, or complex diabetes/vascular disease. Do not start, stop, or change semaglutide without clinician guidance.
  • Clinician discussion warranted: Yes, for anyone using or considering semaglutide who has ocular history or new visual symptoms.
  • Evidence-map change: Yes. Updated GLP-1 entry to retain strong evidence for indicated obesity/diabetes contexts while adding rare ocular-adverse-event monitoring.

docs.title: 3. Anti-inflammatory diet and mental-health outcomes in older adults
docs.text:
• Citation / URL: Mengist B, Lotfaliany M, Pasco JA, Agustini B, Berk M, Forbes M, et al. “Anti-inflammatory diet and mental health outcomes in an aging population: evidence from a preventive population-based target trial emulation.” GeroScience. Published 2026-05-19. DOI: 10.1007/s11357-026-02313-9. https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s11357-026-02313-9
  • Date found: 2026-05-25
  • Evidence type: Target trial emulation using observational data from ASPREE.
  • Population studied: 10,955 community-dwelling adults aged ≥70 years in Australia.
  • Repository score: 8.0/10 — directly older-adult population and clinically relevant mental-health outcomes; downgraded because this is not a randomized diet trial and the effect sizes are modest.
  • Summary: The study emulated a preventive trial comparing an anti-inflammatory dietary pattern with a pro-inflammatory dietary pattern, with depressive symptoms and mental-health quality of life as outcomes.
  • Main finding: An anti-inflammatory diet was associated with lower risk of depressive symptoms (RR 0.92, 95% CI 0.87–0.97) and slightly better mental-health quality-of-life score (β 0.30, 95% CI 0.08–0.52). The combined anti-inflammatory diet/lifestyle contrast had a stronger association with lower depressive symptoms (RR 0.79, 95% CI 0.70–0.89).
  • Relevance to a 74-year-old male: Relevant to cognitive/mental-health resilience and overall healthspan because the cohort is close to the target age and community-dwelling. It supports dietary-pattern attention rather than single-nutrient claims.
  • Limitations: Target trial emulation reduces but does not eliminate confounding. Diet measurement error is possible. Outcomes are depressive symptoms and mental-health quality of life, not dementia, mortality, or physical function.
  • Safety considerations: Not medical advice. Major diet changes should be individualized for weight stability, renal disease, diabetes medications, anticoagulation, swallowing/dental issues, and nutritional adequacy.
  • Clinician discussion warranted: Usually optional for general healthy dietary-pattern questions; warranted if there are chronic diseases, weight loss, medication interactions, or nutritional risks.
  • Evidence-map change: No material change; reinforces the existing strong Mediterranean-style/healthy dietary-pattern category.

docs.title: 4. Online-guided multicomponent physical activity for cognition in healthy older adults
docs.text:
• Citation / URL: Schrenk SJ, Bang C, Best L, Dost T, Flor S, Frahm C, et al. “Impact of an online-guided physical activity intervention on cognition, resting-state brain connectivity, and the gut microbiome in healthy older adults — a randomized controlled trial.” GeroScience. Published 2026-05-21. PMID: 42168724. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/42168724/
  • Date found: 2026-05-25
  • Evidence type: Single-blinded randomized controlled trial.
  • Population studied: 92 healthy older adults, mean age 66.35 years, randomized to 8 weeks of online-guided aerobic/coordination/balance exercise versus active control.
  • Repository score: 7.6/10 — RCT and relevant intervention, but modest sample, younger-than-target mean age, high baseline fitness, and primary endpoint null.
  • Summary: The trial tested whether an online multicomponent physical-activity program improved visual processing speed, other cognitive outcomes, resting-state functional connectivity, cardiorespiratory fitness, or gut microbiome composition.
  • Main finding: The primary outcome, visual processing speed, did not improve versus control. Exploratory findings suggested possible improvements in inhibition and visual memory and increased functional connectivity between visual and dorsal attention networks; no between-group differences were seen in cardiorespiratory fitness or gut microbiome composition.
  • Relevance to a 74-year-old male: Supports the feasibility of structured, remote multicomponent exercise for cognitive-health research, but does not provide strong new evidence that a short online program improves cognition in already-fit older adults.
  • Limitations: Primary endpoint null; exploratory cognitive/neuroimaging results require replication; intervention was short; participants were healthy and relatively young compared with the repository target.
  • Safety considerations: Not medical advice. New moderate-to-vigorous exercise programs should consider fall risk, cardiovascular symptoms, orthopedic limitations, and progression/supervision needs.
  • Clinician discussion warranted: Yes before major exercise intensification or if there is cardiovascular, neurologic, balance, or orthopedic disease.
  • Evidence-map change: No material change; reinforces exercise as a core healthspan intervention but does not alter confidence.

docs.title: 5. Spermidine and vaccine responsiveness in healthy older adults — cautious watchlist item
docs.text:
• Citation / URL: Alsaleh G, Ali M, Kayvanjoo AH, Liu F, Moreau T, Bibi S, et al. “Spermidine Mitigates Immune Cell Senescence and Boosts Vaccine Responses in Healthy Older Adults—A Pilot Study.” Aging Cell. Online issue dated 2026-06; appeared in feed 2026-05-22. DOI: 10.1111/acel.70545. https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/acel.70545
  • Date found: 2026-05-25
  • Evidence type: Double-blind randomized placebo-controlled pilot study.
  • Population studied: 40 adults over age 65 after a third SARS-CoV-2 vaccine dose.
  • Repository score: 7.5/10 as a cautious watchlist item — directly older-adult human RCT with infection-resilience relevance; downgraded for small pilot size, immunologic surrogate endpoints, and need for independent replication.
  • Summary: The study tested daily oral spermidine 6 mg for 13 weeks after vaccination, focusing on tolerability and immune-response biomarkers.
  • Main finding: Supplementation was reported as well tolerated and appeared to improve spike-specific IgG secretion, memory B-cell recall responses, and neutralizing antibody activity specifically among vaccine non-responders, alongside changes in immune-senescence/autophagy markers.
  • Relevance to a 74-year-old male: Potentially relevant to infection resilience and vaccine response in older adults, especially because reduced vaccine responsiveness is a major aging issue. However, this is not yet evidence that spermidine reduces infections, hospitalization, or mortality.
  • Limitations: Very small pilot trial; subgroup signal in vaccine non-responders; surrogate immunologic outcomes; COVID-vaccine context may not generalize to influenza, RSV, pneumococcal, or shingles vaccines.
  • Safety considerations: Not medical advice. Supplements vary in formulation and quality; interactions, renal/hepatic disease, cancer/immunology contexts, and peri-procedural issues should be considered with a clinician. Avoid interpreting this as proof of an anti-aging effect.
  • Clinician discussion warranted: Yes before using spermidine as an immune/longevity supplement, especially with comorbidities or polypharmacy.
  • Evidence-map change: No material change; keep as a watchlist item pending larger trials with clinical infection outcomes.

docs.title: Screened but not included as main updates
docs.text:
• arXiv queries for aging/older adults, longevity/human, frailty/intervention, sarcopenia/exercise, rapamycin/aging, and senolytic/human did not identify new clinically actionable human longevity evidence in the date window.
  • Electroacupuncture plus Otago Exercise Program for sarcopenia (PMID: 42175481) was an older-adult RCT and potentially relevant, but the additive electroacupuncture component, short duration, limited generalizability, and need for replication kept it below the main threshold for changing repository conclusions. It reinforces, rather than changes, the importance of structured exercise for sarcopenia.
  • Biological-age/supplement association studies from recent feeds were not included because cross-sectional epigenetic-clock associations and proprietary supplement signals do not meet the repository threshold for clinically meaningful intervention evidence.

docs.title: Monthly Synthesis — May 2026
docs.text: Educational research support only; not medical advice. This synthesis reviews repository update entries from May 2026, especially the 2026-05-25 update, and summarizes how the month changed the longevity dossier for a 74-year-old male.

docs.title: Source updates reviewed
docs.text: • 2026-05-25 — SGLT2 geriatric-syndrome cohort, semaglutide NAION safety meta-analysis, anti-inflammatory diet target-trial emulation, online exercise RCT, and spermidine vaccine-response pilot.

docs.title: Key findings
docs.text:
1. Cardiometabolic medications remain high-confidence only within standard indications. A large older-adult type 2 diabetes cohort found SGLT2 inhibitor initiation was associated with lower hospitalization and mortality, without an excess geriatric-syndrome signal versus DPP-4 inhibitors. This strengthens confidence in clinician-led use when type 2 diabetes, heart failure, chronic kidney disease, or other standard indications exist, but it does not convert SGLT2 inhibitors into a general longevity drug.
  2. GLP-1 receptor agonists retain strong indicated-use evidence, but safety monitoring matters. A meta-analysis of observational studies found a low-absolute-risk but clinically important semaglutide-associated NAION signal. The finding is not definitive causality, yet it is relevant for older adults with diabetes, vascular risk factors, sleep apnea, ocular history, or new visual symptoms.
  3. Dietary-pattern evidence continues to favor whole-pattern, low-inflammatory/Mediterranean-style approaches rather than supplement-like claims. ASPREE target-trial-emulation data linked anti-inflammatory dietary patterns with modestly lower depressive symptoms and better mental-health quality of life in adults aged 70+.
  4. Exercise remains foundational, but May did not add a field-changing cognition result. An online multicomponent exercise RCT in healthy older adults had a null primary cognitive endpoint, with exploratory signals only. The practical conclusion remains that structured exercise is strongly supported for function and cardiometabolic health, while short remote programs should not be overclaimed for cognition.
  5. Spermidine moved onto a cautious watchlist, not into the evidence map as a proven longevity intervention. A small older-adult pilot RCT suggested possible immune-response benefits after COVID vaccination, especially among vaccine non-responders, but outcomes were surrogate immunologic measures rather than infections, hospitalizations, or mortality.

docs.title: Evidence changes
docs.text:
• SGLT2 inhibitors: Confidence remains strong for standard cardiorenal/metabolic indications and was modestly strengthened for older adults with type 2 diabetes because the new cohort was large, older-adult-specific, and clinically outcome-oriented. Residual confounding prevents a broader longevity claim.
  • GLP-1 receptor agonists / semaglutide: No downgrade for indicated obesity/diabetes use, but the watchlist and evidence map should continue to flag rare ocular safety monitoring.
  • Mediterranean/anti-inflammatory dietary pattern: Reinforces existing strong dietary-pattern category; no major evidence-map change needed.
  • Exercise and cognition: Reinforces exercise as a core intervention but does not materially change confidence for cognitive benefit from short online programs.
  • Spermidine: New cautious watchlist entry is warranted; evidence remains early because the trial was small and biomarker/surrogate-focused.

docs.title: Practical clinician-discussion implications
docs.text:
• For an older man with type 2 diabetes, heart failure, or chronic kidney disease, SGLT2 inhibitor risk/benefit can be a reasonable medication-review topic with a clinician, including hydration, renal function, hypotension, infection risk, and sick-day/perioperative management.
  • For anyone using or considering semaglutide, especially with diabetes, ocular history, sleep apnea, vascular risk factors, or new visual symptoms, the NAION signal is worth clinician discussion. This should not be interpreted as a reason to start, stop, or change medication without medical supervision.
  • Diet quality remains a practical discussion area, especially if weight stability, kidney disease, diabetes medications, anticoagulation, dental/swallowing issues, or frailty risk affect nutritional safety.
  • Exercise programming remains worth individualizing around fall risk, orthopedic limitations, cardiovascular symptoms, and recovery capacity; May's cognition RCT does not justify overpromising cognitive effects.
  • Spermidine may be monitored as a research topic for vaccine responsiveness, but current evidence supports only cautious clinician discussion, not routine self-directed use for longevity.

docs.title: Safety cautions
docs.text:
• This synthesis is educational and is not medical advice.
  • Medication decisions, supplement use, and major diet or exercise changes should be discussed with a qualified clinician, particularly in the setting of polypharmacy, kidney disease, cardiovascular disease, diabetes, frailty, ocular disease, or planned procedures.
  • Observational medication studies can be confounded by indication, prescribing patterns, and baseline health differences.
  • Supplement studies with immune or biological-age biomarkers should not be treated as proof of fewer infections, fewer hospitalizations, slower aging, or longer life.

docs.title: Open questions
docs.text:
• Can SGLT2 inhibitors reduce frailty-relevant outcomes in randomized or quasi-randomized older-adult datasets, beyond standard cardiorenal outcomes?
  • What is the true absolute NAION risk with semaglutide across age, diabetes severity, sleep apnea, and prior ocular disease strata?
  • Which dietary-pattern components drive mental-health and resilience benefits in adults over 70, and are effects clinically meaningful over longer follow-up?
  • Do remote multicomponent exercise programs improve cognition in less-fit or older-than-70 populations when interventions are longer and adequately powered?
  • Does spermidine improve clinically meaningful infection outcomes in older adults, or only vaccine-response biomarkers in selected subgroups?

docs.title: Research Update — 2026-06-01
docs.text: Topic: longevity enhancement / healthspan preservation for a 74-year-old male. Educational research support only; not medical advice.

docs.title: Search notes
docs.text: Sources checked included the configured RSS feeds (Aging Cell, GeroScience, Nature Aging, NIA feed where reachable), PubMed/NCBI for recent clinical literature, Semantic Scholar where available, and arXiv queries for frailty/sarcopenia/geroscience watchlist terms. arXiv did not yield human clinical items meeting the inclusion threshold during this scan. Semantic Scholar was rate-limited during repeated queries, so PubMed and RSS metadata were used for the accepted items.

docs.title: 1. Combined influenza/COVID-19 mRNA vaccine durability in adults ≥50 years
docs.text:
• Citation / URL: Kostanyan L, Wu I, Sinkiewicz M, et al. Safety and durability of influenza and SARS-CoV-2 antibody responses through 6 months after a single dose of mRNA-1083, a multicomponent influenza and COVID-19 vaccine, in adults ≥50 years. Human Vaccines & Immunotherapeutics. 2026. PMID: 42213900. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/42213900/
  • Date found: 2026-06-01
  • Evidence type: Phase 3, observer-blind, active-controlled randomized clinical trial; 6-month safety/reactogenicity and immunogenicity follow-up.
  • Population studied: Adults aged ≥50 years, with a prespecified ≥65-year cohort, randomized to investigational combined mRNA-1083 influenza/COVID-19 vaccine or age-appropriate licensed comparator influenza and COVID-19 vaccines.
  • Rubric score: 8.5/10 — high relevance to older adults, strong trial design, clinically relevant infection-resilience domain, but immunogenicity/safety rather than hard outcomes.
  • Summary: A single dose of mRNA-1083 produced influenza hemagglutination-inhibition titers that were maintained through 6 months and generally comparable to or higher than comparator vaccines. SARS-CoV-2 neutralizing antibody responses remained higher than comparators through 6 months. Reactogenicity was mostly grade 1–2, vaccination-related unsolicited adverse events were similar between groups, and no vaccination-related serious adverse events or deaths were reported through 6 months.
  • Main finding: A combined influenza/COVID-19 mRNA vaccine candidate showed durable humoral responses and acceptable 6-month safety in adults ≥50, including older adults.
  • Relevance to a 74-year-old male: Respiratory infections are a major driver of hospitalization, functional decline, and cardiovascular stress in older men. This supports the broader evidence map category of age-appropriate vaccination and infection resilience, while noting that mRNA-1083 is investigational and not an instruction to choose a specific product.
  • Limitations: Immunogenicity endpoints are not the same as confirmed reductions in hospitalization, mortality, frailty, or functional decline. Follow-up was 6 months; rare adverse events require larger post-authorization surveillance if licensed.
  • Safety considerations / clinician discussion: Vaccination choices should be individualized with a clinician/pharmacist, especially for prior severe vaccine reactions, immune compromise, anticoagulation/injection concerns, or timing with other vaccines. This is not medical advice.
  • Evidence-map change: Yes — added an infection-resilience category emphasizing age-appropriate vaccination as strong evidence, with combined flu/COVID mRNA products remaining product-specific/watchlist until outcome and authorization data mature.

docs.title: 2. Real-time continuous glucose monitoring in type 2 diabetes on basal insulin plus modern therapies
docs.text:
• Citation / URL: Wilmot EG, Moore P, Sathyapalan T, et al. Continuous glucose monitoring versus self-monitoring of blood glucose in individuals with type 2 diabetes: a randomised, multicentre, open-label, superiority trial. The Lancet Diabetes & Endocrinology. 2026. PMID: 42035781. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/42035781/
  • Date found: 2026-06-01
  • Evidence type: Multicenter randomized controlled trial (FreeDM2), open-label, parallel-group superiority design.
  • Population studied: 303 adults with type 2 diabetes treated with basal insulin plus SGLT2 inhibitors, GLP-1 receptor agonists, or dual GIP/GLP-1 receptor agonists; mean age 60.7 years; 67% male; baseline HbA1c about 8.8%.
  • Rubric score: 8.0/10 — high cardiometabolic relevance, RCT design, male-majority cohort; age is somewhat younger than 74 and endpoint is glycemic control rather than mortality/function.
  • Summary: Real-time CGM improved HbA1c compared with self-monitoring of blood glucose. Adjusted HbA1c differences favored CGM at 16 weeks (-0.6 percentage points; 95% CI -0.8 to -0.3) and 32 weeks (-0.5; 95% CI -0.7 to -0.2). Non-device-related adverse events were similar between groups; two severe hypoglycemia events occurred in the control group.
  • Main finding: For adults with type 2 diabetes using basal insulin and modern cardiometabolic agents, CGM improved glycemic control compared with finger-stick self-monitoring.
  • Relevance to a 74-year-old male: If type 2 diabetes and basal insulin are present, better glucose visibility may support safer titration and cardiometabolic risk management. For older adults, the relevance is strongest when balancing glycemic benefit against hypoglycemia risk, treatment burden, dexterity/vision barriers, and individualized HbA1c targets.
  • Limitations: Open-label design; industry funding (Abbott Diabetes Care); mean age 60.7 rather than geriatric-specific; outcomes were HbA1c and safety over 32 weeks, not cardiovascular events, cognition, frailty, or mortality.
  • Safety considerations / clinician discussion: CGM use and insulin titration should be clinician-guided. Older adults may need individualized targets to avoid hypoglycemia, alarm fatigue, overtreatment, or anxiety from data overload. This is not medical advice and does not imply changing diabetes therapy without clinical supervision.
  • Evidence-map change: Yes — diagnostics/tracking now notes CGM as strong for selected insulin-treated type 2 diabetes contexts, not as a general longevity tracker.

docs.title: 3. SGLT2 inhibitor absolute-benefit model for primary heart-failure prevention in type 2 diabetes
docs.text:
• Citation / URL: Young KG, McGovern AP, Hopkins R, et al. Precision Prescribing of SGLT2 Inhibitors in Individuals With Type 2 Diabetes for Primary Prevention of Heart Failure: Model Development and Validation Study. Diabetes Care. 2026. PMID: 42160591. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/42160591/
  • Date found: 2026-06-01
  • Evidence type: Clinical prediction model development and validation using trial meta-analysis estimates plus UK primary-care linked hospital/death records.
  • Population studied: 57,368 SGLT2 inhibitor initiators and 111,673 comparator initiators with type 2 diabetes; focus on individuals without established ASCVD, heart failure, or chronic kidney disease.
  • Rubric score: 7.8/10 — clinically meaningful cardiometabolic prevention question and large validation data, but not an RCT of the model and not older-male-specific.
  • Summary: SGLT2 inhibitor use was associated with a lower risk of new-onset heart failure (HR 0.70; 95% CI 0.63–0.78), consistent with trial evidence. Relative benefit did not vary by baseline absolute heart-failure risk, but predicted 5-year absolute benefit ranged widely (<0.1% to 14.1%; median 1.0%), supporting more individualized targeting.
  • Main finding: Absolute heart-failure benefit from SGLT2 inhibitors in type 2 diabetes varies substantially by baseline risk; a deployable model may better identify who is most likely to benefit for primary prevention.
  • Relevance to a 74-year-old male: Older men with type 2 diabetes often have elevated heart-failure risk, but medication benefit should be framed as absolute benefit versus adverse-event risk. This reinforces clinician-led individualized prescribing rather than treating SGLT2 inhibitors as nonspecific longevity drugs.
  • Limitations: Prediction model and observational validation; residual confounding possible; model transportability outside UK primary-care systems requires evaluation; does not directly address frailty, falls, genitourinary infection, volume depletion, or geriatric outcomes.
  • Safety considerations / clinician discussion: SGLT2 inhibitors require clinician oversight, especially for kidney function, volume status, orthostatic symptoms, genital/urinary infections, ketoacidosis risk, perioperative/sick-day holds, and concurrent diuretics or antihypertensives. This is not medical advice.
  • Evidence-map change: Minor — existing SGLT2 section already strong for indicated cardiometabolic contexts; wording updated to emphasize absolute-benefit targeting in type 2 diabetes.

docs.title: 4. Cardiovascular event timing after COVID-19 across variant eras
docs.text:
• Citation / URL: Howick JF V, Gersh BJ, Scott CG, et al. Time-Critical Cardiovascular Risk After COVID-19: A Population-Based Analysis Across Variant Eras. Mayo Clinic Proceedings: Innovations, Quality & Outcomes. 2026. PMID: 42158619. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/42158619/
  • Date found: 2026-06-01
  • Evidence type: Population-based retrospective cohort study using linked medical records.
  • Population studied: 162,471 adults with SARS-CoV-2 infection from March 2020 through December 2023 in a Minnesota/Wisconsin records-linkage system; age-stratified analyses including adults ≥80 years.
  • Rubric score: 7.6/10 — large cohort and high relevance to older-adult infection/cardiovascular resilience; observational design and variant-era historical context limit causal inference.
  • Summary: Major adverse cardiovascular events, thrombotic events, and dysrhythmias clustered most strongly within 30 days after infection. The early hazard was especially pronounced in older adults during pre-Delta and Delta eras; Omicron-era risk was lower but age-related dysrhythmia risk persisted.
  • Main finding: COVID-19-associated cardiovascular events are concentrated in an early post-infection window, with older adults carrying the greatest absolute vulnerability.
  • Relevance to a 74-year-old male: Reinforces infection prevention, vaccination discussions, and prompt clinician attention to cardiovascular symptoms after respiratory infection. It is most actionable as a monitoring/caution signal, not as a medication recommendation.
  • Limitations: Retrospective observational study; variant eras, vaccination status, treatment availability, and testing behavior may confound results; strongest reported risk was in ≥80-year-olds rather than exactly age 74.
  • Safety considerations / clinician discussion: Chest pain, dyspnea, palpitations, syncope, focal neurologic symptoms, or leg swelling after COVID-19 warrant clinical evaluation. Do not self-start anticoagulants, antiplatelets, or cardiac medications based on this study. This is not medical advice.
  • Evidence-map change: Yes — infection-resilience section now notes post-infection cardiovascular vigilance as moderate observational evidence.

docs.title: Not included / watchlist notes
docs.text:
• Spermidine vaccine-response pilot in older adults appeared again in PubMed/RSS but was already covered in the 2026-05-25 update; no duplicate entry was added.
  • Plant-protein substitution in men at cardiometabolic risk was a randomized crossover feeding study, but the sample was small (19 men; 15 with complete samples) and endpoints were microbiome/metabolomic surrogates. It was logged as interesting nutrition biology but did not meet the ≥7.5 threshold for this repository update.
  • Recent Aging Cell and GeroScience RSS items included several animal/mechanistic or biomarker-focused aging papers. They were not included because they did not provide sufficiently direct human clinical evidence for a 74-year-old male.

docs.text: Sources checked included configured RSS feeds (Aging Cell, GeroScience, Nature Aging, NIA feed where reachable), PubMed/NCBI for recent clinical literature, Semantic Scholar, and arXiv queries for frailty/sarcopenia/geroscience watchlist terms. docs.title: 1. Home-based multifactorial frailty intervention in adults ≥70 years (PromeTheus RCT)
docs.text:
• Citation / URL: Rapp K, Nerz C, Fleiner T, Denkinger M, Büchele G, Rehm M, Mayer B, Rothenbacher D. Home-Based Intervention to Prevent Functional Decline in (Pre)frail Older Adults: The PromeTheus Randomized Controlled Trial. Journal of Cachexia, Sarcopenia and Muscle. 2026. PMID: 42131895. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/42131895/
  • Date found: 2026-06-08
  • Evidence type: Multicenter, assessor-blinded randomized controlled trial; intention-to-treat analysis.
  • Population studied: 385 community-dwelling adults aged ≥70 years with prefrailty/frailty (Clinical Frailty Scale 4–6); mean age 81.2 years; 73.5% female.
  • Rubric score: 8.6/10 — direct older-adult frailty relevance, randomized design, clinically meaningful function/frailty endpoints, clear safety reporting; male-specific certainty is limited by female-majority enrollment.
  • Summary: The 12-month PromeTheus program used existing primary/community-care structures and combined an obligatory unsupervised home exercise program with optional counselling on person-environment fit, nutrition, coping with daily life, and community activities. Compared with usual care, it improved late-life function score (between-group change 1.38 points; 95% CI 0.08 to 2.68), frailty-status improvement (OR 1.72; 95% CI 1.11 to 2.64), and SPPB physical capacity (0.58 points; 95% CI 0.10 to 1.05). It did not significantly improve life-space mobility or fall rate. Participants with lower baseline physical capacity appeared to benefit more. No study-related serious adverse events were reported.
  • Main finding: A pragmatic home-based, multifactorial intervention can modestly improve physical function, physical capacity, and frailty status in community-dwelling prefrail/frail older adults, especially those with lower baseline SPPB.
  • Relevance to a 74-year-old male: Highly relevant if early frailty, mobility limitation, deconditioning, or low SPPB is present. It supports clinician/physical-therapy discussion of structured home exercise plus nutrition/environmental supports rather than relying on exercise advice alone.
  • Limitations: Female-majority cohort; intervention effects were modest; life-space mobility and fall rate were not improved; adherence and implementation quality may vary in real-world settings; unsupervised exercise may not be appropriate for all orthopedic/cardiac risk profiles.
  • Safety considerations / clinician discussion: Not medical advice. Major exercise changes, balance work, or frailty programs should be discussed with a qualified clinician/physical therapist when there is cardiovascular disease, falls, neuropathy, severe arthritis, dizziness, osteoporosis/fracture history, or polypharmacy affecting blood pressure/sedation.
  • Evidence-map change: Yes — added a moderate-confidence line for multidomain frailty interventions and a watchlist/high-confidence topic for frailty screening plus individualized multidomain support.

docs.title: 2. Frailty-related adverse outcomes across systematic reviews and meta-analyses
docs.text:
• Citation / URL: Yu Y, Guo H, Li Z, Lv Z, Chen Y. Frailty-related multiple health outcomes in older individuals: an umbrella review of systematic reviews and meta-analyses. Age and Ageing. 2026. PMID: 42234808. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/42234808/
  • Date found: 2026-06-08
  • Evidence type: Umbrella review of systematic reviews and meta-analyses; PROSPERO-registered protocol.
  • Population studied: Older adults across community, nursing-home, and hospital settings, summarized from 37 systematic reviews/meta-analyses.
  • Rubric score: 8.2/10 — strong synthesis value and very high relevance to geriatric risk stratification; not an intervention trial and many component outcomes had low-to-moderate certainty.
  • Summary: Frailty was associated with multiple adverse outcomes, including mortality, hospitalization, institutionalization, falls, fractures, cognitive decline, and depression. Pooled analyses found suggestive evidence for frailty with mortality (RR 2.07) and prefrailty with mortality (RR 1.38). Cognitive frailty was strongly associated with dementia risk (RR 3.75), multidimensional frailty had the highest mortality risk, and social frailty related to mortality and functional decline.
  • Main finding: Frailty is not merely a descriptive aging label; across evidence syntheses it consistently marks elevated risk across mortality, function, falls/fractures, cognition, mood, and hospital outcomes.
  • Relevance to a 74-year-old male: Supports routinely treating frailty domains — physical, cognitive, social, and multidimensional — as actionable risk markers to discuss with clinicians, especially before major medication changes, surgery, hospitalization, or exercise progression.
  • Limitations: Umbrella reviews inherit heterogeneity and bias from included reviews; many non-pooled outcomes were low-to-moderate certainty; associations do not prove that frailty treatment reverses all downstream risks.
  • Safety considerations / clinician discussion: Not medical advice. Frailty screening can inform conversations about fall prevention, nutrition, medications, cognition, sleep, mood, social support, and rehab, but should not substitute for individualized medical evaluation.
  • Evidence-map change: Minor/yes — reinforced the new multidomain frailty-intervention line and the importance of frailty status as a cross-cutting risk modifier.

docs.title: 3. Semaglutide kidney and survival outcomes in type 2 diabetes with CKD (FLOW subgroup analysis)
docs.text:
• Citation / URL: Tuttle KR, Bakris GL, Baeres FMM, et al. Kidney and Survival Benefits of Semaglutide in Diabetes With Chronic Kidney Disease: FLOW Trial Cardiovascular Subgroup Analyses. Journal of the American College of Cardiology. 2026. PMID: 42233552. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/42233552/
  • Date found: 2026-06-08
  • Evidence type: Prespecified/secondary cardiovascular subgroup analyses from the multicenter randomized FLOW trial.
  • Population studied: 3,533 participants with type 2 diabetes and chronic kidney disease randomized to once-weekly semaglutide 1.0 mg or placebo; subgroups by ASCVD, heart failure, and high predicted cardiovascular risk.
  • Rubric score: 8.0/10 — high-quality randomized disease-specific evidence with kidney/death outcomes; less directly applicable to people without type 2 diabetes and CKD and not a general geroscience/longevity trial.
  • Summary: Semaglutide reduced the primary composite kidney/cardiovascular-death outcome similarly across subgroups with or without ASCVD, heart failure, or high predicted cardiovascular risk. Reported 3-year numbers needed to treat to prevent one primary kidney outcome were 22 with ASCVD, 13 with heart failure, and 17 in the high-risk primary-prevention subgroup. All-cause death point estimates also generally favored semaglutide across subgroups.
  • Main finding: In type 2 diabetes plus CKD, semaglutide’s kidney and survival benefits appeared broadly consistent across baseline cardiovascular-risk categories.
  • Relevance to a 74-year-old male: Relevant only if type 2 diabetes with CKD is present or being evaluated. It strengthens the cardiometabolic evidence base for GLP-1 receptor agonists in standard disease indications, while remaining distinct from unsupported “anti-aging drug” use.
  • Limitations: Subgroup analysis; trial population had type 2 diabetes and CKD, so results should not be generalized to non-diabetic longevity use; frailty, sarcopenia, nutrition, and geriatric tolerability endpoints were not the main focus.
  • Safety considerations / clinician discussion: Not medical advice. GLP-1 receptor agonists require clinician oversight, particularly in older adults with CKD, dehydration risk, gastrointestinal intolerance, gallbladder/pancreatitis history, retinopathy monitoring, weight-loss-related lean-mass loss, and polypharmacy.
  • Evidence-map change: Yes/minor — clarified GLP-1 receptor agonist confidence for indicated type 2 diabetes/CKD contexts while preserving cautions about frailty and monitoring.

docs.title: 4. Comparative safety signals for SGLT2 inhibitors, GLP-1 receptor agonists, and DPP-4 inhibitors in Danish registries
docs.text:
• Citation / URL: Lund LC, Simonsen E, Pottegård A. Comprehensive drug safety assessment of sodium-glucose co-transporter 2 inhibitors, glucagon-like peptide 1 receptor agonists, and dipeptidyl peptidase 4 inhibitors: A Danish population-based active comparator new user cohort study. Diabetes Research and Clinical Practice. 2026. PMID: 42202888. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/42202888/
  • Date found: 2026-06-08
  • Evidence type: Danish nationwide population-based active-comparator new-user cohort safety study.
  • Population studied: 49,365 SGLT2 inhibitor initiators, 33,018 GLP-1 receptor agonist initiators, and 50,531 DPP-4 inhibitor initiators.
  • Rubric score: 7.7/10 — large real-world safety study relevant to common cardiometabolic drugs; observational safety-signal design means residual confounding is likely.
  • Summary: The study screened 43 adverse-event associations among newer glucose-lowering therapies. Reported signals included higher phimosis and secondary polycythaemia with SGLT2 inhibitor initiation versus comparators; higher bacterial intestinal infections and anaemia with DPP-4 inhibitor initiation; and a near-immediate uterine cancer signal with GLP-1 receptor agonists that the authors noted may reflect residual confounding. The authors concluded that several infection and cancer signals warrant further investigation.
  • Main finding: Real-world registry surveillance can identify safety signals that should shape monitoring discussions, but the signals are not proof of causality.
  • Relevance to a 74-year-old male: For an older man with diabetes indications, the male-relevant SGLT2 signals (genital/urinary complications and polycythaemia) reinforce careful clinician monitoring. The uterine cancer signal is not male-relevant but illustrates confounding concerns in observational drug-safety screens.
  • Limitations: Observational design; many screened outcomes raise multiple-comparison and residual-confounding concerns; registry codes may misclassify events; not designed to estimate net longevity benefit.
  • Safety considerations / clinician discussion: Not medical advice. Medication choices should be clinician-directed with monitoring for kidney function, volume status, genital/urinary infections, blood counts when clinically indicated, GI effects, nutritional status during weight loss, and sick-day/perioperative plans.
  • Evidence-map change: No major confidence change — added cautionary detail to the watchlist context rather than changing the overall evidence grade for indicated uses.

docs.title: 5. Influenza vaccine effectiveness in adults ≥60 years across three seasons in Jiaxing, China
docs.text:
• Citation / URL: Liu Y, Fu X, Wang Y, Jia L, Xu L, Li R, Zhu G, Zhou H. Evaluation of influenza vaccine effectiveness among older adults in Jiaxing, China, 2022-2025: A test negative design-based study. Human Vaccines & Immunotherapeutics. 2026. PMID: 42202217. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/42202217/
  • Date found: 2026-06-08
  • Evidence type: Test-negative case-control vaccine-effectiveness study across three influenza seasons.
  • Population studied: 3,214 patients aged ≥60 years with acute respiratory symptoms in Jiaxing, China, 2022–2025.
  • Rubric score: 7.6/10 — older-adult infection-resilience relevance and clinically meaningful laboratory-confirmed influenza endpoint; observational regional design and seasonal strain variation limit generalizability.
  • Summary: Influenza vaccination was associated with moderate protection against laboratory-confirmed influenza, with adjusted vaccine effectiveness of 42.5% (95% CI 31.2% to 51.9%). Effectiveness was higher in the 2024–2025 season (47.2%), against A(H1N1) (42.4%), and within 90 days post-vaccination (52.3%).
  • Main finding: Recent real-world data continue to support moderate influenza vaccine protection in older adults, with timing and seasonal match likely important.
  • Relevance to a 74-year-old male: Reinforces the evidence-map conclusion that age-appropriate vaccination is a high-confidence infection-resilience measure for older adults, especially because influenza can precipitate hospitalization, functional decline, and cardiovascular events.
  • Limitations: Regional data from China may not generalize to other countries, vaccine products, strains, or seasons; test-negative design reduces but does not eliminate confounding; study did not directly measure frailty, cardiovascular events, or mortality.
  • Safety considerations / clinician discussion: Not medical advice. Vaccine timing/product choice should be discussed with a clinician/pharmacist, especially with prior severe vaccine reactions, immune compromise, anticoagulation/injection concerns, or coordination with COVID-19/RSV/pneumococcal vaccination.
  • Evidence-map change: No — reinforces the existing strong infection-resilience vaccination category.

docs.title: Not included / watchlist notes
docs.text:
• Aging Cell RSS items from the scan were mostly mechanistic, animal, cellular-senescence, or biomarker papers (for example mitochondrial stress in aged mouse muscle, vascular immune remodeling, and liver biological-age scoring). They were not included because they did not provide sufficiently direct clinical intervention evidence for a 74-year-old male.
